structure.title: Preface
structure.summary: The partial document is a structured guide titled “The Complete Guide to Building Skills for Claude.” Its table of contents outlines the key sections, indicating a comprehensive approach to skill development for the Claude platform. The guide begins with an introductory overview, followed by foundational concepts in the “Fundamentals” section. It then moves into practical aspects of creating skills, covering “Planning and Design,” “Testing and Iteration,” and “Distribution and Sharing.” Additional support is provided through “Patterns and Troubleshooting,” and the document concludes with a curated list of “Resources and References” for further learning. Overall, the partial document presents a step‑by‑step framework for designing, refining, and deploying effective Claude skills.

structure.title: Introduction
structure.summary:
The excerpt introduces “skills” as packaged instruction folders that teach Claude specific tasks or workflows, enabling repeatable, customized automation without repeatedly re‑explaining preferences. It highlights typical use cases such as frontend design generation, research methodology, document styling, and multi‑step process orchestration, and notes how skills complement Claude’s native capabilities (code execution, document creation) and MCP integrations.  

The guide promises a complete walkthrough—from planning and structuring skills, through testing and distribution, to practical patterns for both standalone skills and MCP‑enhanced workflows. It outlines learning objectives (technical requirements, design patterns, testing, iteration, distribution) and identifies the intended audience: developers, power users, and teams seeking consistent, standardized Claude behavior.  

Two learning paths are presented: one for building standalone skills and another for enhancing MCP integrations, sharing the same technical foundations. The expected outcome is the ability to create a functional skill in 15‑30 minutes using the skill‑creator. The document then transitions into Chapter 1: Fundamentals.

structure.title: Fundamentals
structure.summary: The excerpt introduces the concept of a “skill” as a structured folder containing required Markdown instructions (with YAML front‑matter) and optional scripts, references, and assets. It outlines core design principles—progressive disclosure (three‑level loading to minimize token use), composability (skills must work alongside others), and portability (skills run unchanged across Claude.ai, Claude Code, and the API). The text then relates skills to MCP (connectors), using a kitchen analogy: MCP supplies tools and infrastructure, while skills provide the recipes (workflows and best practices). It contrasts the user experience without skills (confusing, inconsistent, higher support load) versus with skills (automated, consistent, guided interactions). Finally, it signals the next section will cover planning and design.

structure.title: Planning and design
structure.summary: The excerpt is a guide on planning and designing Anthropic “skills.” It begins by stressing the importance of defining 2‑3 concrete use cases before coding, illustrating a detailed sprint‑planning example and prompting the creator to consider user goals, multi‑step workflows, required tools, and domain knowledge. It then outlines three common skill categories—Document & Asset Creation, Workflow Automation, and MCP Enhancement—each with real examples and key techniques. The next section defines success criteria, offering quantitative benchmarks (trigger rate, tool‑call count, API‑error rate) and qualitative goals (no user prompting, consistent results). Technical requirements follow, specifying the required file layout, naming conventions, and the critical YAML front‑matter format (name and description fields). Detailed rules for the front‑matter (prohibited characters, reserved names) and guidance on writing effective descriptions are provided, with examples of good and bad descriptions. The document then describes how to write the main skill instructions in SKILL.md, offering a template for steps, examples, and troubleshooting, and concludes with best‑practice tips such as being specific, including error handling, referencing bundled resources, and using progressive disclosure. The passage ends just before the next chapter on testing and iteration.

structure.title: Testing and iteration
structure.summary: The excerpt outlines Chapter 3, “Testing and iteration,” describing a tiered approach to skill testing—manual testing in Claude.ai, scripted testing in Claude Code, and programmatic testing via the skills API—along with guidance on choosing the appropriate level of rigor. It emphasizes iterating on a single challenging task first, then expanding coverage, and presents a recommended testing framework divided into triggering tests, functional tests, and performance comparisons. The passage also introduces the “skill‑creator” tool for generating, reviewing, and refining SKILL.md files, and details how to iteratively improve skills based on feedback signals such as under‑triggering, over‑triggering, and execution issues.

structure.title: Distribution and sharing
structure.summary: The excerpt outlines the distribution and sharing model for Claude AI “skills” as of January 2026. It explains how individual users acquire skills (download, zip, upload via Claude.ai settings or place in the Claude Code directory) and how admins can deploy them organization‑wide with automatic updates. It introduces the open “Agent Skills” standard for portable, cross‑platform skills, notes platform‑specific compatibility tags, and describes API‑based skill management ( /v1/skills endpoint, container.skills parameter, version control, integration with the Claude Agent SDK) versus using skills through the Claude.ai UI. A decision matrix shows when to use the UI (end‑user interaction, testing, ad‑hoc workflows) versus the API (programmatic, large‑scale, automated pipelines). The document then recommends a workflow for publishing skills on GitHub (public repo, clear README, examples), linking them in MCP documentation, and providing installation guides. Finally, it offers guidance on positioning a skill—emphasizing outcome‑focused messaging and highlighting the combined value of MCP integration and skills.

structure.title: Patterns and troubleshooting
structure.summary:
The excerpt is Chapter 5 of a guide on building and maintaining “skills” that orchestrate MCP (Managed Cloud Platform) tools. It first explains two design mind‑sets—problem‑first (focus on the user’s outcome) versus tool‑first (focus on an already‑connected service)—and how each influences skill architecture. It then presents five reusable patterns, each with a typical use case, sample workflow steps and “key techniques”:

1. **Sequential workflow orchestration** – ordered multi‑step processes with dependencies, validation, and rollback.  
2. **Multi‑MCP coordination** – linking several MCP services across distinct phases (e.g., design‑to‑development hand‑off).  
3. **Iterative refinement** – loops that improve output quality via validation scripts and stop criteria.  
4. **Context‑aware tool selection** – decision trees that pick the optimal storage or processing tool based on file type, size, etc., with transparent rationale.  
5. **Domain‑specific intelligence** – embedding specialist logic (e.g., compliance checks) before invoking a tool, plus audit logging.

The latter half of the chapter is a troubleshooting manual. It lists common errors (missing SKILL.md, malformed YAML front‑matter, invalid skill names), symptoms (skill not loading, over‑triggering, MCP call failures, ignored instructions, sluggish performance), and step‑by‑step fixes or checklists for each. Guidance includes naming conventions, description tuning, negative triggers, MCP connection verification, instruction clarity, and strategies for reducing large‑context load (splitting documentation, limiting enabled skills). The section ends with a brief segue to Chapter 6, which contains resources and references.

structure.title: Resources and references
structure.summary:
The excerpt is a developer‑focused guide for creating Claude “skills.” It lists the primary resources (Best Practices Guide, API reference, MCP docs, Anthropic blog posts) and points to the public GitHub skills repository. It introduces the built‑in **skill‑creator** tool for generating and reviewing skills, and explains how to get support via Discord and GitHub issues. The document includes three reference sections:  

* **Reference A – Quick checklist** that outlines pre‑development, in‑development, pre‑upload, and post‑upload validation steps (use‑case identification, folder naming, required files, YAML front‑matter format, testing, monitoring).  
* **Reference B – YAML front‑matter schema** that specifies required fields (name, description) and optional metadata (license, allowed‑tools, author, version, tags, etc.), plus security restrictions (no XML tags, no code execution, reserved prefixes).  
* **Reference C – Complete skill examples** linking to production‑ready skill templates for document generation, workflow patterns, and partner integrations (Asana, Atlassian, Canva, Figma, Sentry, Zapier, etc.).  

Overall, the passage serves as a consolidated resource hub and checklist for building, validating, and publishing Claude skills.
